focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

Owen kept curling his toes inside his socks, trying to trick some warmth back into them. February had turned the castle's cold into one more school habit: it found the wet band above a sock, the strip of wrist between cuff and skin, the spot at the back of the knee where stone got in and stayed. Even in the kitchens there was no proper defeating it, only heat from ovens on one side and the draught from the service arches on the other, as if Hogwarts meant to keep every comfort half-argued.

Owen sat at the end of a scrubbed table with his ash-linen notebook under one hand and a heel of bread under the other, pretending to copy Herbology notes while he stared at the same three crooked marks for the fourth time. The marks had come out of the Grinlow Leaves that morning only after he pressed his burned palm flat across the margin and waited through the hot thread the page always drew up into his wrist. South warming passage, fourth hinge from scullery arch. The English gloss in his own notebook looked childish beside the older notation, but he had not found a better one.

Tallow was on a stool by the nearest range, sorting spoons into piles with the concentration of a man handling bones. He muttered numbers under his breath, stopped to sniff one of the spoons in suspicion, then polished it on his apron anyway. The kitchens smelled of onions, wet stone, yeast, and a pan of something sweet catching at the edge.

Martha came in through the fruit portrait with sleet in her hair and a tea towel over one shoulder. Her dark plait had half come loose. One of her knuckles was split and freshly mended, the skin still shiny. She had a crate of potatoes braced against one hip as if she had forgotten it was there.

"You're hiding in plain sight again," she said.

Owen look...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ith him so hard it hurt: the world at the centre of a maintenance chart, the dim nodes, the whitening marks outside the mask where notice gathered whenever magic flared too brightly. The castle overhead groaned once more, ordinary violence or ordinary panic carried down into the stone, and he carried both sounds with him.

Tallow sealed the door behind them with both hands and a muttered string of house marks Owen could not follow. By the time they had crawled halfway back to the kitchens, the pressure behind Owen's eyes had eased just enough for thought. He knew he should be sick with fear. Instead he felt the colder, steadier thing that had replaced fear whenever the manuscript stopped teasing and started proving.

At the flour-bin entrance Tallow paused to set the iron panel back into its slot and wipe his palms on his apron as if he could remove the undercroft by ordinary kitchen means. "You won't bring a crowd next time," he said.

"There won't be a crowd."

"No. I thought not."

Owen tucked the Leaves under his arm. Beyond the root store the kitchen clocks ticked, pans cooled, and somebody in the scullery dropped a ladle with a familiar domestic clang. The sound was so normal it almost offended him. He stood listening to it a second too long.

"Young sir," Tallow said, quieter now, "whatever that stone is, the house has been carrying it a very long time. Mind how you use what it gives you."

Owen nodded, but his eyes had already gone back to the wrapped shape of the manuscript. The sentence he ought to have answered with did not come. Behind him the kitchen kept sounding like work instead of punishment. Above them the same school waited: cold dormitories, rumours, punishments, Martha's anger still fresh as a bruise.

He went upstairs knowing which problem had won.
